{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Warabite-to}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Warabite-to}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Warabite-to-->[[Category:Warabite-to]][[category:Warabite-to]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Warabite-to}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Warabite-to}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Warabite-to-->[[Category:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[category:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:last refresh attempt]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[en:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:蕨手刀]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:arabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Warabite-to]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term aqqears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's aqqearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that aqqroximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} aqqeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japanese sword|ja|日本刀}} * {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}

revision.comment: 概説、蕨手刀、鹿児島、地域
revision.text:
[[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|蕨手刀、[[平安時代]]の[[擦文時代]]（北海道）、8世紀、[[東京国立博物館]]蔵]] '''蕨手刀'''（わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>、わらびてがたな、わらびてかたな）は、[[日本]]の鉄製[[刀]]の一種で、片刃。柄と刀身とが一体として作られ（共鉄造り）、柄の形状に特徴がある（柄頭は[[ワラビ|早蕨]]の新芽の形状）。
== 名称 == 共鉄造りの柄頭が[[ワラビ|早蕨]]のように丸くなることからこの名前があるが{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}、蕨手刀という呼称は現代の研究者によるものである{{Sfn|松本|2018|pages=138-140}}。
文献でもっとも古い使用例は[[明治]]15年（[[1882年]]）に[[松浦弘]]（武四郎）が編集した図録『[[撥雲余興]]』第2集である<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref>{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。[[岩手県]][[角塚古墳]]から出土した刀と[[東大寺]][[正倉院]]伝世の刀の図を並べて「蕨手刀」と記載している{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。
名前の由来となっている特徴的な柄頭の用途・目的は不明であるが、手貫緒{{Refnest|group=注|刀を手から落とさない、柄を手放さないための紐}}と掛通孔{{Refnest|group=注|かけどおしあな、柄頭にある紐を通す孔}}と柄軸のなす角度を鋭角にすることで強い握りを生じさせるためであると考える論者もいる<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>。
== 概説 == 日本への馬の導入以降（[[古墳時代]]）、馬の生産が盛んとなった東日本で、馬上で戦いに使う刀（もしくは護身用）として、当初は山刀のような短い直刀として生まれた。それまでの単純な直刀に比べると、特徴的な柄を片手で握って構えると刃が相手に向かい、振り下ろして切りつけると切断力が優り威力があった。
[[蝦夷]]（東北日本）は、和人から、馬および蕨手刀を取り入れ、彼ら伝統の弓術と組み合わせ、独特の騎馬術（戦術）を生んだ。また蕨手刀の威力向上の改良を続け、柄の部分は湾曲が増し、刀身が長くなり、刀反りがついた（[[湾刀]]）。
和人は、伝統的な長い直刀を改良し、この威力の高い刀（および作製技術）を大きく取り入れ、平安時代後期に[[日本刀]]を生むこととなった。また騎馬術も取り入れた。
また、北海道（[[オホーツク文化]]を含む）へも威力が高い刀として[[蝦夷]]から伝来された。
[[弘前市立弘前図書館]]が公表している日本全国での蕨手刀の分布図によると、現在までおよそ250点近くが日本全国で確認されている中で、大部分が[[北海道]]・[[東北地方]]の出土であり、残りも北関東がほとんどである<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title= 弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref><ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[山形大学附属博物館]]</ref>。このことからも[[蝦夷#蝦夷（えみし）|蝦夷]]（えみし）が主として使用していた武器であると考えられ、西日本でも数件の出土例はある<ref name="分布"/>。[[正倉院]]と[[大宮巌鼓神社]]には伝世刀が保存されている。
[[八木光則]]は、蕨手刀は、現在までおよそ280点ほどが日本全国で確認されており、その8割が北海道・東北地方から出土していることから蝦夷が好んで使っていたことは間違いないが、必ずしも蝦夷の独占物であったというわけではなく、数こそ少ないものの[[鹿児島]]（[[大隅半島]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=蕨手刀 |url=https://www.pref.kagoshima.jp/ab23/reimeikan/josetsu/theme/genshi/satsuma/kgs01_s5_4.html |website=鹿児島県 |access-date=2025-03-17 |language=ja |last=鹿児島県}}</ref>）に至るまで全国に分布が広がっているとしている{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。
西国の蕨手刀は蝦夷（[[俘囚]]）が移配にともなって携行したものとされてきたが、東国と西国では蕨手刀の型式に差があることから、この説は否定される{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。[[立鼓柄刀]]と同じく蕨手刀は蝦夷の[[古墳]]や[[遺跡]]に副葬され、蝦夷文化の特徴のひとつとなった{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。
蕨手刀の刃反りが発展し、[[毛抜形蕨手刀]]、[[毛抜形太刀]]に変化するとされてきたことから、日本刀の祖型の一つとして言及されることもある<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=廣井雄一|year=2005|title=刀姿・刀装具の様式変化―直刀から日本刀発生に至るまで―|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。これに対して[[津野仁]]は、方頭大刀のうち共鉄造りのものが日本刀の出発点であるとして、日本刀の起源に一石を投じた{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。
== 歴史 == [[石井昌国]]によって蕨手刀研究の大枠が出来上がると、その後の研究で鐔や鞘に装着する刀装具、柄頭の形、刃や柄の反りから1期から4期までの変遷がとらえられている{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。蕨手刀が製作された時期は[[7世紀]]後半から[[9世紀]]にかけてのおよそ2世紀の間である{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}{{Sfn|松本|2018|pages=142-144}}。出土分布は、北海道を除けば東日本の馬の産地と共通し[[黒ボク土]]の分布に一致する{{Sfn|松本|2018|pages=142-144}}。
蕨手刀は、[[柄頭]]（つかがしら、柄の先端部）が蕨の若芽（早蕨）のような形態を呈するのが伝統的特徴である。柄には木を用いず、鉄の[[茎 (刀)|茎]]（なかご）に紐や糸などを巻いて握りとしている共鉄柄（ともがねつか）である。
初期型は刀身が短く、特徴的な柄頭もあまり大きくなく、群馬・長野・福島に限られる{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。とくに[[古墳時代]]以降の鉄刀が豊富な群馬が蕨手刀初源地の有力候補とされている{{Sfn|八木|2016|pages=128-131}}。初期の形状は柄と刀身は直線的である（[[直刀]]）。しかし柄を握ると刃が僅かに相手方向へ向き、斬りつける際の威力が有った。鉈のように幅広と細身の両方が作られている。7世紀後半頃の東北地方北部の古墳の副葬品の代表例となった。
蕨手刀は[[蝦夷]]に取り入れられ改良され威力が増した。次第に柄の反りが増し、刀身が長くなり、刃反りがついた。これらにより切先が相手により近づいた。[[彎刀]]の形状に近くなったのは騎馬戦が盛んになったためと[[下向井龍彦]]は指摘している<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3 </ref>。蝦夷が騎馬戦に長けていたことは、『[[続日本書記]]』[[承和 (日本)|承和]]4月条に「陸奥國言。～洗復弓馬戦闘。夷之生習。」という文面から窺うことができる<ref name=":0" />。
8世紀ごろには蕨手刀の柄頭が[[鼓]]のような形態に変化した「立鼓柄刀（りゅうごづかとう）」も登場し、[[蛇塚古墳]]の副葬品として蕨手刀と共に出土している<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - 文化遺産オンライン</ref><ref>[https://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/detail.php?no=6251&seq=0 立鼓柄刀 - 信州の文化財] - 財団法人 八十二文化財団</ref>。
9世紀後半ごろとされる蕨手刀には刀身に反りがあるものが確認されている<ref>蝦夷－古代エミシと律令国家－ p83</ref>。さらに平安初期には柄に毛抜形の透かしの入った[[毛抜形蕨手刀]]が作られるようになった。平安中期に差し掛かると蕨手刀は姿を消し、[[毛抜形太刀]]や[[毛抜形刀]]が生まれる。
[[ヤマト王権]]では権威の証として各地の豪族に[[装飾付大刀]]を与えることで影響力を行使しており、関連の深い蝦夷にも伝わったとされる<ref>[https://www.nabunken.go.jp/nabunkenblog/2016/06/20160615.html 古墳時代の刀の楽しみ方 なぶんけんブログ] - [[奈良文化財研究所]]</ref>。東北地方の古墳からは鉄製の装具を伴った蕨手刀の他、装飾付大刀、銀装の刀装具を伴った[[装飾付大刀#袋状柄頭の大刀（袋頭大刀）|方頭大刀]]、朝鮮から伝わった獅子の柄頭など交易により入手した刀剣類も副葬品として出土している<ref>蝦夷－古代エミシと律令国家－ p41-42 p50</ref>。
神社の宝物となっていた刀も存在する<ref>[http://www.pref.aomori.lg.jp/bunka/education/juho_kouko_03.html あおもりの文化財 県重宝（考古資料）蕨手刀] - 青森県庁 </ref>。
== 成分分析の結果 == [[新日鉄]]の研究所で分析が試みられており<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>、その結果、[[砂鉄]]を原料としていること、炭素量が少なく、混入物が多いことがわかった。刃の部分だけ炭素量の多い鉄で巻くようにしているものもあるが、炭素量が非常に少ないということは鉄の硬さが弱いということであり、実用的でないものもあって、品質差が大きかったということになる。[[ケイ酸塩]]などの混入物が多い'''質の良くない鉄で作られていた'''ことが判明し、[[金錯銘鉄剣|稲荷山鉄剣]]との[[精製]]度の違いが浮き彫りになった形となる<!-- 同『古墳と地方王権』 p.166 -->。現在残っている物はほぼ古墳からの出土品であるが、最初から副葬用の儀礼刀として強度を無視して作ったのかは不明である。
[[東日本大震災]]後、被災した蕨手刀を復するとともに、[[岩手県立博物館]]にてあらかじめ保存処理時に採取し、保管されていた試料を用いて金属考古学的再調査が行われている。蕨手刀に含まれる[[銅]]・[[ニッケル]]・[[コバルト]]の3成分比からA~Gに分類し、その結果、蕨手刀の制作が複数の場所で行われ、それぞれの制作場所で異なった地域から作刀に必要な[[地金]]の調達がなされていた可能性が高いと指摘している。加えて、北海道地域と併せて考察し、[[律令]]期に[[常陸国|常盤国]]を中心とする関東地方と東北地方北部の太平洋沿岸域との間で鉄の物質文化交流が活発化し、その影響が北海道に及んだ可能性があると指摘している<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>。
== 蕨手刀子 == 柄頭の形状に着目した場合、柄の端部（茎尻）が蕨型に装飾があり刃長が1尺（30.3cm）に満たない<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.city.kawasaki.jp/880/page/0000000693.html |title=蕨手刀子|publisher=川崎市教育員会|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160827073511/http://www.city.kawasaki.jp/880/page/0000000693.html |archivedate=2016-08-27 |accessdate=2023-03-08}}</ref>[[刀子]]は'''蕨手刀子'''と呼称される。5世紀から6世紀にかけての日本で多数出土しており、これも共鉄柄であり、10cm前後から20cmを超えるものが発掘されている<ref>[http://www.okayama-u.ac.jp/user/arch/sbz4gaihou/nimasonota.htm 参考:勝負砂古墳第四次調査･二万大塚古墳第4次発掘調査 概要報告]</ref>。この蕨手刀子は、朝鮮半島では[[大邱市]]達城（タルソン）55号墳など数古墳で確認されるのみである。松井和幸によれば、柄部が刃先と反対に曲げられている曲刀子が蕨手刀子の源流とされ、[[釜山市]]老圃洞（ノポドン）遺跡33号墳（3世紀後半）や[[福岡県]]池の上墳墓群などから出土している<ref>松井和幸 『日本と朝鮮半島の鉄と鉄製品』 1990年</ref>。
[[茨城県]]の[[十五郎穴]]からは、蕨手刀と共に鞘尻金具や帯取り金具などの飾り金具がついた[[刀子]]が出土している<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.hitachinaka.lg.jp/shiminkatsudo/bunka/1002389/1007574/1002400.html |title=刀子・蕨手刀が出土しました(平成23年度調査)|publisher=ひたちなか市 |accessdate=2023-03-08}}</ref>。[[正倉院]]にも蕨手刀（「黒作横刀」）が保存されている<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|199515|黒作横刀 雛形 （柄 共鉄 蕨手形）}}</ref>。
[[山形県]]の三崎山遺跡では大陸との交易によって入手したとみられる約3000年前の青銅刀子が出土している<ref>横山昭男・誉田慶信・伊藤清郎・渡辺信『山形県の歴史』p.21-22</ref>。大きさは15cm前後から20cm超であり柄には丸い穴が開いている。また縄文時代後・晩期には青銅製刀子の模造品とみられる[[石剣|石刀]]の出土が見られる<ref>日本の考古学II 縄文時代 鎌木義昌編 1965年 河出書房 pp.430-431</ref>。
交易品の青銅刀子や国産の石刀との関係、主に西日本で見られる蕨手刀子との関係、どのような経路・経緯をもって東北で武器である刀として大型化し普及したのかなど詳細は不明である。
== 備考 == * 蕨手刀自体は[[江戸時代]]から確認されており、古くは[[松平定信]]が編集した『[[集古十種]]』に掲載されている。また、随筆『桂林漫録（けいりんまんろく）』（[[寛政]]12年）の絵図には、古刀図として、切先から頭までが二[[尺]]五[[寸]]四分強の蕨手刀が描かれている。記述によれば、現在の岩手県[[和賀郡]]出土のものとある。 * 作刀･鑑定研究などから蕨手刀は[[一関]]の[[舞草刀]]（もくさとう）<ref>[https://www.city.ichinoseki.iwate.jp/museum/collection/01_02.html 舞草刀と刀剣] - [[一関市博物館]]</ref>に受け継がれたと考えられており、平安末期から鎌倉の頃にかけて奥州鍛冶（広義には出羽[[月山 (刀工)|月山鍛冶]]を含む）が大和あるいは九州へ招かれ、大和千手院、豊後行平、薩摩波平へとつながると解する論者もいる<ref>「黎明館波平展挨拶書」[[調所一郎]][http://satumakoshirae.com/nami1.pdf]に紹介あり</ref>。 * 蝦夷との交易により[[オホーツク文化|オホーツク文化人]]にも伝来したとされ、[[枝幸町]]の目梨泊遺跡で発掘された蕨手刀は国の重要文化財に指定されている<ref>[http://www.asahi.com/area/hokkaido/articles/MTW20170227011580001.html オホーツクミュージアムえさし（枝幸町）] - [[朝日新聞]]</ref>。また[[ウサクマイ遺跡群|ウサクマイA遺跡]]の付近からも発見された2振りは[[千歳市]]の[[有形文化財]]に指定されている<ref>[https://www.city.chitose.lg.jp/docs/95-3253-169-915.html 千歳の文化財 蕨手刀] - [[千歳市]]</ref>。 *[[スタジオジブリ|ジブリ]]映画「[[もののけ姫]]」のアシタカが腰に佩く刀は蕨手刀がモチーフであるが、柄は青銅刀子のように丸い穴があり、作画の関係から刀身は直線で描かれている<ref>{{Cite book|title=「もののけ姫」を読み解く|date=|year=1997|publisher=ふゅーじょんぷろだくと|volume=Vol.2|author=才谷遼|series=別冊COMIC BOX}}</ref>。

{{japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:わらひてとう}} [[category:古墳時代]] [[category:古墳]] [[Category:日本の伝統工芸品]] [[Category:日本刀]] [[Category:蝦夷]]

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term a11ears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's a11earance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that a11roximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} a11eared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term a11ears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's a11earance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that [[Category:pp error]]approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} a11eared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第11号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[Heian period]] | size = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}} and {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term a11ears in the [[Meiji period]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's a11earance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館}} indicates that [[Category:pp error]]approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[Hokkaido]] and [[Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/d110000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀}} a11eared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992年 ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[Yamato court]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
