<!--Warabite-to--><!--Warabite-to--><!--Warabite-to--><!--Warabite-to--><!--Warabite-to--><!--Warabite-to--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Traditional Craft Items of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Warabiteto}} [[ko:궐수도]] [[zh:蕨手刀]] {{wikidata link|Q11621998}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|qq=|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

[[ja:蕨手刀]]
<!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Traditional Craft Items of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Warabiteto}} [[ko:궐수도]] [[zh:蕨手刀]] {{wikidata link|Q11621998}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = [[File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg|thumb|300px|Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]]] | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] }}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|qq=|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] |caption=Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]}}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|qq=|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Japanese sword|Japanese sword]] * [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]]

The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|qq=|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Warabiteto]][[ja:蕨手刀]][[ko:궐수도]][[zh:蕨手刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] |caption=Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]}}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>

The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].

While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />

By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>

The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|qq=|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

<!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Traditional Craft Items of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Warabiteto}} [[ko:궐수도]] [[zh:蕨手刀]] {{wikidata link|Q11621998}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Non matching pagename on wikidata]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Warabiteto]][[ja:蕨手刀]][[ko:궐수도]][[zh:蕨手刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] |caption=Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]}}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

[[ja:蕨手刀]]
<!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--><!--Warabite Katana--> [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Traditional Craft Items of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Warabiteto}} [[ko:궐수도]] [[zh:蕨手刀]] {{wikidata link|Q11621998}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Non matching pagename on wikidata]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Warabiteto]][[ja:蕨手刀]][[ko:궐수도]][[zh:蕨手刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] |caption=Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]}}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Warabiteto]][[ja:蕨手刀]][[ko:궐수도]][[zh:蕨手刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11621998]] Wikidata name: warabitetō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Warabiteto | image = File:蕨手刀, Warabiteto, 8th century.jpg | alternate_names = | origin = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | type = Iron blade | material = Steel | historical_period = [[Kofun period]] to [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] | area = Variable, one-sided blade | location = Various locations in Japan, primarily found in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]] and [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tohoku region]] |caption=Warabiteto, [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], found in the [[Satsumon period]] (Hokkaido), 8th century, housed at [[Tokyo National Museum]]}}
'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. The handle and blade are crafted integrally (tomogane construction), with the handle shaped like the budding sprout of a fern (warabi).
== Etymology == The name "Warabite-tō" derives from the distinctive rounded pommel of its hilt, which resembles young bracken shoots. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
The name derives from the handle's rounded design, reminiscent of fern sprouts (warabi), a feature identified in contemporary research.<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The earliest recorded mention of this term is found in the illustrated catalog ''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' edited by {{ill|Matsura Hiro|ja|松浦弘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsura Hiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松浦武四郎|1=Matsuura Takeshirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531839}} in 1882.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532}}</ref>
The earliest documented use of the term appears in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1882) in the catalog "''{{ill|Hatsuun Yoki|ja|撥雲余興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsuun Yoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''," edited by [[Matsuura Hiroshi]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=蕨手刀の型式分類及び編年と分布|date=|year=2008|publisher=六一書房|series=地域と文化の考古学Ⅱ|author=黒済和彦}}</ref> The name reflects the pommel's appearance, although its original purpose is unclear. Some scholars suggest it may have improved grip strength or served as an attachment point for straps (tekan-o) or lanyards (kakedōshi ana).<ref>{{Cite journal|author=鈴木信|year=2005|title=北・東日本の出土刀にみる彎刀の起源|journal=考古学ジャーナル|volume=No.532|page=}}</ref>
== Overview == Following the introduction of horses to Japan during the [[Kofun period]], Warabite-tō emerged as a mounted weapon in [[Eastern Japan]]. Its single-edged blade was well-suited for slashing attacks from horseback, a significant improvement over earlier straight swords.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan adopted Warabite-tō and incorporated their traditional archery into unique cavalry tactics. Over time, they enhanced the sword's design, introducing curved hilts and blades (wan-tō).
Warabite-tō gradually influenced the development of [[Japanese sword|Japanese swords]]. By the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it contributed to the evolution of [[Kenukigata-tō]] and eventually the iconic [[Japanese katana]].
The Warabiteto emerged as a weapon for horseback combat or self-defense, primarily in Eastern Japan during the [[Kofun period]]. Compared to earlier straight-edged swords, its distinctive design allowed for effective cutting power when swung downward.
The [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] incorporated the Warabiteto into their martial traditions, combining it with their archery skills to develop unique cavalry tactics. Over time, the handle curvature increased, and the blade lengthened, leading to curved designs (wan-to). These developments influenced later Japanese swords, including the [[tachi]] and [[katana]].
== Distribution == A distribution map compiled by the {{ill|Hirosaki City Library|ja|弘前市立弘前図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki City Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前市立図書館|1=Hirosaki City Public Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60988531}} indicates that approximately 250 examples of Warabite-tō have been identified across Japan, with the majority discovered in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] regions.<ref name="分布">{{Cite web|和書|title=弘前市立弘前図書館 古代の生産活動と生活用具(9)蕨手刀|url=https://adeac.jp/hirosaki-lib/text-list/dpp0000/ht030400|accessdate=2021-10-25}}</ref> A smaller number have been found in northern [[:en:Kanto|Kanto]], and a few examples are distributed as far as western Japan.<ref>[http://museum.yamagata-u.ac.jp/rekishikouko.html#anchor-warabitetou 蕨手刀] - [[Yamagata University Museum]]</ref>
While primarily associated with the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]], the spread of Warabite-tō throughout Japan indicates that it was not exclusively their weapon. Differences in design between eastern and western variants suggest regional adaptations rather than a single source of dissemination.<ref name="分布" />
== Historical Development == Warabite-tō underwent several design changes over time. Early examples featured straight blades and small pommels, likely originating in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]. As mounted warfare gained prominence, the hilts and blades of Warabite-tō became more curved, increasing their effectiveness in slashing attacks.<ref>日本の歴史07『武士の成長と院政』 2001st year 下向井龍彦 [[:en:講談社|講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3</ref>
By the late 8th century, a variant known as the {{ill|Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja|立鼓柄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūgotsuka-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appeared, featuring drum-shaped pommels. Both Warabite-tō and its variants were frequently included as burial goods in [[Kofun period]] tombs, particularly in northeastern Japan.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/255155/2 立鼓柄刀] - [[Cultural Heritage Online]]</ref>
In the 9th century, Warabite-tō designs evolved to include partially curved blades, reflecting the influence of mounted combat. By the early Heian period, Warabite-tō were replaced by {{ill|Kenukigata-tō|ja|毛抜形蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tō|1=kenukigata warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547736}} and {{ill|Kenukigata-tachi|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}, precursors to the modern Japanese sword.
== Metallurgical Analysis == Metallurgical studies conducted by [[:en:Nippon Steel Corporation|Nippon Steel Corporation]] reveal that Warabite-tō were made primarily from low-quality iron derived from [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]]. While some examples feature higher carbon content in their blades for increased durability, significant variations in quality suggest that many were primarily ceremonial.<ref>『古墳と地方王権』 [[Kobayashi Saburo|小林三郎]]編 [[新人物往来社]] 1992nd year ISBN 4-404-019769 p.166</ref>
Recent research suggests that Warabite-tō were produced at multiple locations using regionally sourced materials. A 2014 study also highlights active trade in iron between [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]] and northeastern Japan during the Ritsuryō period.<ref>{{Cite book|title=被災蕨手刀からみえてきた鉄の物質文化交流|date=|year=2014|publisher=ニュー・サイエンス社|issue=662|series=考古学ジャーナル|author=赤沼英男}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The Warabiteto was not only a functional weapon but also a cultural artifact, reflecting the Emishi's adaptation of technology from the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]]. Historical sources indicate its symbolic use in rituals and its association with military prowess.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:柏木東遺跡 (茂漁第pp号墳出土) 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from {{ill|Kashiwagi East Site|ja|柏木東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwagi East Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Eniwa City, Hokkaido (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:伝長野県長和町大門出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto reportedly excavated from Daimon, Nagawa Town, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:長野県東御市祢津出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nezu, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) File:徳島県吉野川市鴨島町敷地西ノ宮出土 蕨手刀.JPG|Warabiteto excavated from Nishinomiya, Shikiji, Kamojima Town, Yoshinogawa City, Tokushima Prefecture (Exhibited at Tokyo National Museum) </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Japanese sword|Japanese sword]] * [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]]

'''Warabiteto''' (蕨手刀, わらびてとう<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>, わらびてがたな, わらびてかたな) is a type of Japanese iron sword characterized by a single-edged blade. However, this term was coined by modern researchers.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|和書|title=八戸市の丹後平古墳群出土品が国重文に 末期古墳で初指定|url=https://hachinohe.keizai.biz/headline/763/|website=八戸経済新聞|accessdate=2021-05-23}}</ref>
